diagrams.title: SMS Messaging Architecture in Android
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing how SMS messages flow from application to recipient through Android's telephony system
elements: Application Layer (SMS App) - Top box showing user interface
SmsManager API - Rectangle below app with sendTextMessage() method
SMS Provider (Content Provider) - Cylinder shape representing SQLite database storage
Telephony Framework - Medium box with telephony services
RIL (Radio Interface Layer) - Bridge component connecting to hardware
Baseband Processor/Modem - Bottom hardware component with antenna icon
Mobile Network - Cloud outside device with SMSC (Short Message Service Center)
Arrows showing message flow downward and delivery confirmation upward
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand the complete SMS message pathway from user input to network transmission, including where SQLite database stores messages locally on the device

diagrams.title: Location Based Services Architecture in Android
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing Android location system components and data flow from hardware to application
elements: LBS Applications (Top layer) - Multiple app icons representing different LBS apps
LocationManager API - Central service block with requestLocationUpdates() method
Three Location Provider columns - GPS Provider (satellite icon), Network Provider (cell tower icon), Passive Provider (reuse icon)
Hardware Layer - GPS Chip (satellite dish), Cell Towers (multiple towers), WiFi Access Points
LocationListener interfaces - Arrow connections showing callback delivery to apps
Geocoding Service - Separate external service box with address translation
Location Permissions box - ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION
diagrams.purpose: Shows students how Android abstracts different location sources through the LocationManager, enabling apps to request location data without worrying about underlying hardware specifics and provider differences

diagrams.title: SMS Send/Receive Process Flow
diagrams.description: Detailed flowchart showing complete SMS transmission and reception process in Android
elements: Start: User composes message and presses send
Process box: SmsManager.sendTextMessage() called
Decision diamond: Valid phone number? (Yes/No branches)
Process: Message stored in SMS Provider (SQLite) with PENDING status
Decision diamond: Network available? (Yes branch to network, No to queue)
Process: RIL forwards message to Baseband processor
Process: Message transmitted to SMSC (SMS Center) via mobile network
Process: SMSC routes to recipient's device
Process: Delivery report (ACK) sent back through network
End: Message status updated to DELIVERED in database
Receiver path: BroadcastReceiver listens for SMS_RECEIVED action
Process: PDU parsing extracts message content
Process: Message stored in inbox via ContentProvider
End: Notification displayed to user
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the complete SMS lifecycle from composition through network delivery and reception, helping students understand error handling, confirmation mechanisms, and database state changes

diagrams.title: Location Update Request and Delivery Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing how Android delivers location updates from providers to applications
elements: Start: App requests location updates via LocationManager
Decision diamond: Fine location permission granted? (Yes/No with error handling)
Process: Criteria object specifies accuracy requirements
Process: LocationManager.requestLocationUpdates() called with provider, interval, distance
Provider selection: GPS Provider (high accuracy), Network Provider (balanced), Passive Provider (reuse)
Process: Hardware acquires satellite fix or network triangulation
Process: LocationListener.onLocationChanged() callback triggered
Process: Location object created with latitude, longitude, altitude, accuracy
Arrow delivers location to application
End: App updates UI or processes location data
Continuous loop: Updates continue until app calls removeUpdates()
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand the asynchronous nature of location updates, how criteria affect provider selection, and the importance of properly managing location requests to conserve battery

charts.title: SMS vs MMS Comparison
charts.content: SMS (Short Message Service) supports plain text only with 160 character limit per segment, uses SMSC for routing, has lower cost per message, wider device compatibility, and uses SmsManager API. MMS (Multimedia Messaging Service) supports text, images, audio, video with up to 300KB-1MB, uses MMS centers, has higher cost, limited carrier support, and uses MmsManager API.

charts.title: Location Providers Comparison
charts.content: GPS Provider uses satellite signals with 5-10 meter accuracy outdoors, high battery consumption (100mA+), slow Time to First Fix (30-120 seconds), requires clear sky view, and best for navigation. Network Provider uses cell towers and WiFi with 50-500 meter accuracy, low battery consumption (10-30mA), fast response (<5 seconds), works indoors, and best for city navigation. Passive Provider reuses other app location data with variable accuracy, minimal battery usage, instant response, depends on other apps, and best for background location.

charts.title: Android SMS Permission Flow
charts.content: User initiates SMS action in app, app checks for required permissions (SEND_SMS, RECEIVE_SMS, READ_SMS, READ_PHONE_STATE), if not granted show rationale dialog, user grants via system permission dialog, if granted proceed with SmsManager operation, if denied show error message and disable SMS feature, successful operations update SMS Provider database, errors caught and user notified.

charts.title: Location Based Services Classification
charts.content: Tracking Services include fleet management, personal locator, asset tracking, fitness tracking with GPS routes. Navigation Services include turn-by-turn GPS navigation, indoor positioning, augmented reality navigation, parking finder. Information Services include nearest POI (Points of Interest), weather updates, geofencing alerts, location-based reminders. Social Services include location sharing, check-ins, friend proximity alerts, location-based gaming.

branches: SMS Sending - SmsManager API, sendTextMessage(), sendMultipartTextMessage(), destination address, sc address
SMS Receiving - BroadcastReceiver, SMS_RECEIVED action, SMS_DELIVERED action, PDU format parsing
Permissions - SEND_SMS, RECEIVE_SMS, READ_SMS, READ_PHONE_STATE, runtime permission checks
Data Storage - SQLite via SMS Provider (content://sms/), inbox/sent/draft tables, ContentResolver operations
Telephony System - TelephonyManager for call info, PhoneStateListener, SIM card status, network type detection
Error Handling - SmsException, UnknownError, SMS failed status codes, delivery report processing
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview of SMS implementation in Android, covering essential APIs, manifest permissions, data persistence via SQLite ContentProvider, and integration with the broader telephony system for exam preparation

branches: Location Providers - GPS_PROVIDER, NETWORK_PROVIDER, PASSIVE_PROVIDER with different characteristics
LocationManager API - requestLocationUpdates(), getLastKnownLocation(), getProviders(), setCriteria()
LocationListener Interface - onLocationChanged(), onProviderEnabled(), onProviderDisabled() callback methods
Location Class - getLatitude(), getLongitude(), getAccuracy(), getAltitude(), getSpeed(), getBearing() methods
Geocoding - Geocoder class, getFromLocation() for latitude/longitude to address, getFromLocationName() for address to coordinates
Geofencing - Geofence API, GeofenceBroadcastReceiver, circular geofence regions with transition types ENTER, EXIT, DWELL
Use Cases - Real-time tracking, location sharing, proximity alerts, fitness apps, navigation apps
mindMaps.purpose: Organizes LBS components and APIs into structured learning aid covering all essential classes, interfaces, and practical implementation considerations for building location-aware Android applications
